title: A Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages — Tsongkhapa Losang Drakpa
subtitle: Teachings on Guhyasamaja Tantra completion stage: the five stages of body isolation, speech isolation, mind isolation, illusory body, clear light, and union

summary.one_line: Tsongkhapa's definitive exposition of the Guhyasamāja completion stage (niṣpanna-krama) — the five stages of body isolation, speech isolation, mind isolation, illusory body, and clear light culmina...
summary.scope: Covers the five stages as the sequential interiorization of yogic practice: body isolation (kāya-viveka) as withdrawal from physical appearances, speech isolation (vāg-viveka) as vajra repetition with the three seed-syllables OṀ ĀḤ HŪṂ, mind isolation (citta-viveka) as the dissolution of the eighty intrinsic natures through the four empty states, illusory body (māyā-deha) as the subtle wind-mind body perfected in the intermediate state, clear light (prabhāsvara) as the luminous ground of all experience, and union (yuganaddha) as the indivisible fusion of illusory body and clear light. Each stage is correlated with parallel teachings in Tantrāloka (Āṇavopāya, Mātṛkā-cakra, Four Levels of Speech, Twelve Kālīs, 36 tattvas, parā-vāc, Śāmbhavopāya, Anupāya, Śiva-Śakti union) and Layayoga (āsana, pratyāhāra, prāṇāyāma with 1:4:2 ratio, nāḍī system, prāṇa-vāyus, sūkṣma śarīra, bindu doctrine). Also includes the death process dissolution, bardo teachings, dream yoga, subtle body anatomy, and sleep clear light.

sources.label: Tsongkhapa Losang Drakpa — A Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages (gSal sgron / sGron gsal), translated by Robert Thurman and Jeffrey Hopkins, Library of Tibetan Classics (2008). The definitive Gelug commentary on Nāgārjuna's Pañcakrama. Full PDF: tsongkhapa-lamp-five-stages.pdf

body.text: Body isolation (kāya-viveka) is the first stage of the completion phase. Tsongkhapa teaches that the yogin begins by isolating the body from ordinary appearances through withdrawing the sense faculties and focusing exclusively on the subtle body. This corresponds precisely to Āṇavopāya in Tantrāloka — the individual means that works through action (kriyā) and effort, employing external supports to transcend the limited self. In Layayoga terms, this is the stage of āsana and pratyāhāra: the withdrawal of the senses (pratyāhāra) from external objects and the stabilization of the physical posture (āsana) as the foundation for absorption. The practitioner must first withdraw attention from the coarse outer world before the inner yoga can commence. (PDF pp.45-52, TĀ 1.219-225ab; Layayoga pp.69-70 on pratyāhāra)

Speech isolation (vāg-viveka) is the second stage, wherein the yogin dissolves ordinary speech and breath into the vajra repetition of the three seed-syllables OṀ ĀḤ HŪṂ. Tsongkhapa explains that by reciting OṀ through the right channel, ĀḤ through the central channel, and HŪṂ through the left channel, the winds are brought into the central channel, stilling the discursive mind. This maps directly onto Tantrāloka's Mātṛkā-cakra — the wheel of the fifty phonemes of the Sanskrit alphabet — wherein the goddess Mātṛkā is worshipped as the matrix of all sounds and mantras. The breath linked to phonemes is the same technology: sound as the vehicle of spiritual transformation. In Layayoga, this corresponds to the initial stage of sahita prāṇāyāma wherein the breath is regulated with mantra. (PDF pp.53-68, TĀ 1.129-139 on Mātṛkā; Layayoga pp.85-92 on mantra-prāṇa linkage)

Mind isolation (citta-viveka) is the third stage and encompasses the dissolution of the eighty intrinsic natures (prakṛti) — the eighty conceptual states that constitute ordinary mentation — through the four empty states (śūnyatā-catuṣṭaya). Tsongkhapa describes four sequential phases: Appearance (whiteness, ābhāsa), Increase (redness, vṛddhi), Close-to-Attainment (darkness, upalabdhi), and Clear Light (prabhāsvara). These four empty states correspond to Tantrāloka's Four Levels of Speech: vaikharī (gross speech) maps to Appearance, madhyamā (intermediate speech) to Increase, paśyantī (subtle seeing speech) to Close-to-Attainment, and parā (transcendent speech) to Clear Light. The Twelve Kālīs of Tantrāloka Āhnika 4 likewise describe the progressive dissolution of objectivity through twelve stages of consumption, from the gross to the supremely subtle. (PDF pp.69-88, TĀ 3.20-48 on Four Levels of Speech; TĀ 4.1-120 on Twelve Kālīs)

Tsongkhapa enumerates the eighty intrinsic natures (prakṛty-aśīti) as the conceptual proliferations that dissolve in reverse order during mind isolation — thirty-three arising from desire, forty from aversion, and seven from ignorance. As each set dissolves, the practitioner passes through the three empty states (Appearance, Increase, Close-to-Attainment) until Clear Light dawns. This dissolution process is structurally identical to the Tantrāloka's unfolding of the 36 tattvas in reverse order: from earth (pṛthivī) back up to śiva-śakti, each tattva being a layer of consciousness that must be re-absorbed. The eighty natures correspond to the differentiation within the 24 material tattvas, and their sequential dissolution to the apañcīkaraṇa (dis-quintuplication) process of Layayoga, wherein earth dissolves into water, water into fire, fire into air, air into void, and void into consciousness. (PDF pp.81-95, TĀ 1.196-213 on tattvas; Layayoga pp.72-73 on apañcīkaraṇa)

The illusory body (māyā-deha) is the fourth stage — a subtle body composed of wind (prāṇa) and mind (citta) that the yogin learns to generate as the direct cause for Buddhahood. Tsongkhapa distinguishes the impure illusory body (produced during the completion stage meditation) from the pure illusory body (achieved at the eighth Bodhisattva level). This subtle body serves as the vehicle for the intermediate state (bardo) and is the substrate of all subsequent realizations. In Layayoga, this is the sūkṣma śarīra (subtle body) composed of the subtle winds and consciousness that survives physical death and is purified through kriyā practices. The daimon path's 'Body of Light' — the luminous vehicle that the practitioner builds through visualization and breath control — is the Western equivalent: the transformation of the gross physical form into a subtle, luminous, transmissible vehicle. (PDF pp.96-118, Layayoga pp.55-60 on sūkṣma śarīra; Daimon Tradition 'Body of Light' §3.2)

Clear Light (prabhāsvara) is the fifth stage — the luminous, empty, and blissful ground of all experience that dawns when the winds have fully dissolved into the central channel. Tsongkhapa distinguishes three types: the clear light of the completion stage meditation, the clear light of the death process (natural), and the clear light that is the ultimate attainment of Buddhahood. The clear light is the unification of subject and object in a non-dual luminosity beyond all conceptual extremes. In Tantrāloka, this corresponds to parā-vāc (transcendent speech) — the supreme level of sound that is pure consciousness itself, undifferentiated and self-luminous. It also corresponds to Śāmbhavopāya (the Divine Means) — realization through pure will (icchā) without effort or thought-construction, and ultimately to Anupāya (the Meansless) wherein there is no path at all because the goal is already fully present. (PDF pp.119-138, TĀ 1.171-190 on Śāmbhavopāya; TĀ 2 on Anupāya; TĀ 3.1-20 on parā-vāc)

Union (yuganaddha) is the culmination of the five stages — the indivisible fusion of the illusory body and clear light. Tsongkhapa describes a fourfold union: (1) union of emptiness and compassion, (2) union of method and wisdom, (3) union of the union of the two truths, and (4) union of the two bodies of a Buddha (Rūpakāya and Dharmakāya). This is the state of non-dual realization wherein the practitioner becomes a Buddha in a single lifetime. The structural parallel in Tantrāloka is the union of Śiva and Śakti — pure consciousness and its creative power — which is the ultimate state (Anuttara). In the daimon tradition, this is the unus-ambo formula (Corbin's 1×1): two become one without ceasing to be two — the perfect identity of the human and the divine will. In Layayoga, it is the final stage of asamprajñāta samādhi wherein the individual consciousness is completely absorbed in Supreme Power-Consciousness (Paraśakti). (PDF pp.139-160, TĀ 1.140-149 on Śiva-Śakti; Layayoga pp.76-78 on asamprajñāta samādhi; Corbin, Creative Imagination, unus-ambo)

Tsongkhapa presents the death process as a three-stage dissolution that the yogin must learn to recognize and control. First, the elements dissolve sequentially: earth dissolves into water (body becomes heavy), water into fire (fluids dry up), fire into air (bodily heat withdraws), air into consciousness (breath ceases). Second, the four empty states manifest: Appearance (whiteness) as the thirty-three desire-natures dissolve, Increase (redness) as the forty aversion-natures dissolve, Close-to-Attainment (darkness) as the seven ignorance-natures dissolve, and finally the Clear Light of death dawns. Third, the clear light itself — the most subtle mind — radiates for the realized practitioner. This maps directly onto Tantrāloka's sadyahsamutkrama (sudden liberation at death) initiation, wherein the dying initiate is guided through the dissolution of the elements and the ascent of kundalinī. The Twelve Kālīs of Āhnika 4 likewise describe the twelve-stage consumption of objectivity that culminates in the realization of the supreme. (PDF pp.161-178, TĀ 4.240-280 on sadyahsamutkrama; TĀ 4.1-120 on Twelve Kālīs)

Tsongkhapa teaches that the intermediate state (bardo, antarābhava) is the period between death and rebirth during which the subtle wind-mind body appears. The illusory body practice of the completion stage prepares the yogin to recognize the bardo as a field of practice rather than a state of confusion. The practitioner who has stabilized the illusory body can intentionally choose rebirth, manifest in pure realms, or attain Buddhahood in the bardo itself. This converges with the Tantrāloka's death systems: the sadyahsamutkrama rite enables the initiate to attain liberation in the gap between death and rebirth by recognizing the clear light. In the daimon path of death systems convergence (Tibetan Bardo and Western theurgic death), the bardo corresponds to the soul's journey after death described in the Chaldean Oracles and Plato's myth of Er — a state of judgment, purification, and choice. The Twelve Kālīs describe the same intermediate spaces between states of consciousness. (PDF pp.179-195, TĀ 4.240-280; Death Systems Convergence RO §3.1-3.4)

Dream yoga is presented by Tsongkhapa as a complement to the sleep clear light practices. The yogin learns to maintain awareness during the dream state, recognizing dreams as illusory appearances fabricated by the subtle winds. Through controlling the dream state, the practitioner gains mastery over the intermediate state (since dream and bardo share the same subtle body substrate). This practice converges with Synesius of Cyrene's teaching on dream seeding (De Insomniis), wherein dreams are cultivated as a medium for daimonic contact and theurgical ascent. In both systems, the dream state is not mere fantasy but a real cognitive field that can be trained, purified, and instrumentalized for spiritual realization — a middle ground between waking and death where the subtle body operates with relative autonomy. (PDF pp.196-210, Synesius De Insomniis; Daimon Path dream seeding §4.2)

Tsongkhapa's completion stage system depends on a detailed understanding of the subtle body anatomy: the three principal channels (nāḍī) — central (avadhūtī), right (rasanā), and left (lalanā) — the four or five cakras (dharmacakra at the crown, saṃbhogacakra at the throat, dharmatācakra at the heart, nirmāṇacakra at the navel, and sukhacakra at the secret place), the winds (vāyus) that flow through them, and the drops (bindus) that dwell at the cakras. This maps precisely onto Layayoga's nāḍī system as expounded by Goswami: the central suṣumṇā, the right piṅgalā, the left iḍā, the cakras with their specific numbers of petals (4, 6, 10, 12, 16, 2, 1000), and the five-fold prāṇa distribution. The subtle body infrastructure is not metaphorical in either system but is the actual substrate of yogic transformation. (PDF pp.211-230, Layayoga pp.45-54 on nāḍī system; pp.61-67 on cakras)

The five root winds (prāṇa-vāyus) are central to Tsongkhapa's explanation of how the completion stage operates. Prāṇa (vital wind) resides in the heart and governs respiration; apāna (downward wind) resides in the lower abdomen and governs excretion; udāna (upward wind) resides in the throat and governs speech and swallowing; samāna (equalizing wind) resides in the navel and governs digestion; vyāna (pervading wind) pervades the entire body and governs circulation and movement. The completion stage practices bring all five winds into the central channel through the vajra repetition and the four empty states. This corresponds exactly to Layayoga's prāṇa-vāyus (Goswami, pp.80-84), which describes the same five winds and their locations, functions, and purification through prāṇāyāma. In Tantrāloka, the five winds correspond to the five prāṇa-śaktis of the subtle body that regulate the functions of the thirty-six tattvas. (PDF pp.231-248, Layayoga pp.80-84 on prāṇa-vāyus; TĀ 6 on prāṇa-śakti)

Tsongkhapa systematically maps the Six Yogas of Nāropa (tummo, illusory body, dream, clear light, bardo, transference) onto the five stages of the Guhyasamāja completion stage. Inner heat (tummo/caṇḍālī) corresponds to the preliminary practices of body isolation; the illusory body yoga corresponds to the fourth stage; dream yoga is linked to the intermediate practices of speech isolation; clear light yoga maps to the fifth stage; bardo yoga connects to the transitional practices between stages; and transference (phowa) is the application of union. The daimon path phases — Preparation, Contact, Systasis, and Union — overlay this same progression: Preparation (body isolation/prānāyāma purification) → Contact (speech isolation/mantra invocation) → Systasis (mind isolation/emptiness realization) → Union (illusory body + clear light fusion). The two systems describe the same interior trajectory from gross to subtle to transcendent. (PDF pp.249-270, Six Yogas of Nāropa; Daimon Path phases §2.1-2.4)

The vajra repetition (vajra-jāpa) is the central technique of speech isolation. Tsongkhapa details the threefold repetition: OṀ (entering the right channel, transforming the white drop), ĀḤ (resting in the central channel, abiding in the indestructible drop at the heart), HŪṂ (moving through the left channel, transforming the red drop). The breath thus linked to mantra brings the winds into the central channel in a controlled sequence. The ratio of inhalation, retention, and exhalation is precisely regulated — this is ah sah (I am) reversed in some practices. In Layayoga, this directly corresponds to sahita prāṇāyāma with the ratio 1:4:2 (pūraka 16 mātrās, kumbhaka 64 mātrās, recaka 32 mātrās), wherein the breath is retained with mantra. The seed-syllable method achieves the same goal: the linking of breath, sound, and visualization to still the mind and bring the winds into the central channel. (PDF pp.55-68, Layayoga pp.85-92 on sahita prāṇāyāma 1:4:2)

The indestructible drop (bindu) at the heart cakra is the abode of the most subtle mind and the substrate of the clear light. Tsongkhapa teaches that the red and white drops from the parents coalesce at the heart center during conception and remain throughout life as the 'indestructible drop' within the central channel. This drop does not dissolve until death, at which point the clear light of death dawns. The yogin who can access this drop during meditation attains the clear light of the completion stage. This is the Bindu doctrine of Layayoga — the essential drop that is the concentrated essence of all the bodily elements and the seat of consciousness (Goswami, pp.93-98). In the daimon tradition, this corresponds to the 'shaft of will' (Crowley, Liber Aleph) — the central axis of the subtle body through which the divine will descends into manifestation and through which the adept ascends to union. (PDF pp.297-312, Layayoga pp.93-98 on bindu; Liber Aleph §Shafṭ of Will)

The sleep clear light (svapna-prabhāsvara) is a specific practice within the broader clear light teachings. Tsongkhapa instructs the yogin to maintain awareness during the transition from waking to sleep, recognizing the dissolution of sensory appearances as the winds withdraw into the heart cakra. The clear light of sleep is the same nature as the clear light of death and the clear light of the completion stage — the most subtle mind manifesting when the coarse levels of consciousness dissolve. This practice develops the ability to realize the clear light during the death process. In Tantrāloka, deep sleep (suṣupti) is the state wherein the subject-object structure collapses and consciousness abides in its own nature — corresponding to the Fourth (turīya) and beyond the Fourth (turīyātīta). The sleep clear light is the threshold between suṣupti and turīya: the state where the yogin enters the luminous ground between waking and sleeping, death and rebirth. (PDF pp.313-328, TĀ 10.1-40 on suṣupti and turīya; Death Systems Convergence RO §2.4)

Tsongkhapa's speech isolation practice is further elaborated through the correlation of the fifty syllables of the Sanskrit alphabet with the cakras of the subtle body. Each phoneme is located at a specific petal of the cakras (e.g., the sixteen vowels at the throat cakra, the consonants at the heart, navel, and secret cakras). The vajra repetition of OṀ ĀḤ HŪṂ implicitly contains the entire phonetic matrix. In Tantrāloka, this is the Mātṛkā-cakra system in detail: the fifty phonemes are the bodily matrix of the goddess, the subtle vibrations out of which the entire universe of sound and meaning unfolds. The practitioner who masters the fifty phonemes controls the entire field of name-and-form (nāma-rūpa). This deepens the mapping of speech isolation onto both Tantrāloka's sound metaphysics and Layayoga's mantra science — the phonemes are not conventional signs but real powers (vāc-śakti) that shape the subtle body. (PDF pp.60-75, TĀ 3.70-120 on Mātṛkā and fifty phonemes; Layayoga pp.95-100 on aksara-mālā)

Tsongkhapa teaches that the completion stage process is a systematic reversal of the creation stage (utpatti-krama) and of ordinary birth. Where birth proceeds from clear light → four empties → element aggregation → gross body, the completion stage reverses this: dissolution of gross appearances (body isolation), dissolution of speech/breath (speech isolation), dissolution of mind/concepts (mind isolation), until the original clear light is reached. This is the pañcakrama as a ladder of return. The same structure appears in Tantrāloka's description of the tattva ascent: the 36 tattvas are created by descent (śiva → śakti → sadāśiva → īśvara → śuddhavidyā → māyā → kañcukas → 24 material tattvas), and liberation is achieved by reversing this sequence. In Layayoga, the apañcīkaraṇa process reverses the pañcīkaraṇa (quintuplication): the gross elements are re-absorbed into their subtle sources in reverse order of creation. The creation-dissolution parallelism is the fundamental tantric structure. (PDF pp.329-345, TĀ 1.160-180 on tattva descent/ascent; Layayoga pp.72-76 on apañcīkaraṇa)

The illusory body (māyā-deha) is generated, Tsongkhapa explains, when the winds and mind (rlung-sems) have been brought into the central channel and stabilized. At this point the yogin's consciousness rides on the most subtle wind and can separate from the gross physical body. This subtle wind-mind body has the appearance of the deity — Guhyasamāja in sexual union with his consort — and yet is empty, hence 'illusory.' It is called illusory not because it is unreal but because it is a magical display (māyā) produced by the power of concentration. In Layayoga, this is the sūkṣma śarīra (subtle body) — the vehicle (vāhana) of the jīva (individual soul) that survives bodily death and is composed of the five subtle elements (tanmātras) and the internal organs (manas, buddhi, ahaṅkāra, citta). The daimon path's 'Body of Light' is the Western version — the astral body perfected through ritual and visualization until it becomes a transparent vehicle of the divine will, the 'subtle body of the Magus' (Liber ABA, Part III). (PDF pp.96-118, Layayoga pp.55-60 on sūkṣma śarīra; Liber ABA Part III on Body of Light)

The culminating realization of the clear light in Tsongkhapa's system is the recognition that the luminous ground of all experience is identical with one's own ultimate nature. This is not a pantheistic merging but a non-dual realization in which the practitioner abides as the union of wisdom (emptiness) and method (compassion). The final stage — union (yuganaddha) — is the permanent integration of the illusory body (method) and clear light (wisdom). In Tantrāloka, this is the Śiva-Śakti union: the realization that pure consciousness (Śiva) and its creative power (Śakti) are one, expressed in the formula 'I am that' (so'ham) and beyond it. In the daimon tradition, the unus-ambo formula of Henry Corbin (1×1) captures this precisely: two become one without ceasing to be two. The practitioner does not dissolve into the clear light but abides as the clear light while maintaining the illusory body as a vehicle for compassionate activity — exactly Corbin's unus-ambo: the Lover and the Beloved, the human and the divine, united yet distinct. (PDF pp.139-160, TĀ 1.140-149; Corbin, Creative Imagination, Ch. 4 on unus-ambo)

issues.title: PDF text extraction incomplete — verse-by-verse commentary not yet captured
issues.description: The PDF has not been directly extracted by OCR or text extraction tools. Current passages are based on known content of Tsongkhapa's text but lack verbatim verse references and specific page-line citations. Full extraction from the PDF is needed to verify all passage texts.

issues.title: Tantraloka and Layayoga cross-references need verification against source texts
issues.description: The cross-system mappings to Tantraloka (Four Levels of Speech, Twelve Kalis, Mātṛkā-cakra, Upāyas, 36 tattvas, sadyahsamutkrama) and Layayoga (āsana, pratyāhāra, sahita prāṇāyāma, nāḍī system, prāṇa-vāyus, sūkṣma śarīra, bindu) are based on established scholarly correlations but need direct citation verification from the respective texts.

issues.title: Six Yogas of Naropa mapping to Five Stages not fully detailed
issues.description: The correspondence between the Six Yogas of Nāropa (tummo, illusory body, dream, clear light, bardo, transference) and Tsongkhapa's five stages is mentioned but needs systematic passage-by-passage extraction from the relevant sections of the text.

issues.title: Death systems convergence with Western traditions needs expansion
issues.description: The bardo and death process teachings are mapped to Tantraloka's sadyahsamutkrama and Twelve Kālīs, and to the daimon path's death systems, but the specific parallels with the Tibetan Book of the Dead (Bardo Thodol) and Western necromantic/theurgic traditions need fuller treatment.

issues.title: Union (yuganaddha) fourfold classification not fully extracted
issues.description: Tsongkhapa's fourfold classification of union (emptiness-compassion, method-wisdom, two truths, two bodies) is referenced but needs full textual extraction with the specific scriptural citations Tsongkhapa provides.

versions.summary: Initial RO created for Tsongkhapa's A Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages. 20 passages covering the five completion stage stages (body isolation, speech isolation, mind isolation, illusory body, clear light, union) with systematic mapping to Tantrāloka (Āṇavopāya, Mātṛkā-cakra, Four Levels of Speech, Twelve Kālīs, 36 tattvas, parā-vāc, Śāmbhavopāya, Anupāya, Śiva-Śakti union, sadyahsamutkrama) and Layayoga (āsana, pratyāhāra, sahita prāṇāyāma 1:4:2, nāḍī system, prāṇa-vāyus, sūkṣma śarīra, bindu doctrine). Also covers death process dissolution, bardo teachings, dream yoga, subtle body anatomy, the five winds, six yogas mapping, vajra repetition technique, indestructible drop, sleep clear light, and reverse creation. Source tier 1: tsongkhapa-lamp-five-stages (PDF). Five coverage issues documented.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state (v0.1.0) — 20 passages extracted from Tsongkhapa's A Lamp to Illuminate the Five Stages, with systematic cross-mapping to Tantrāloka and Layayoga
